facts.title: K-pop tops the world charts
facts.fact: K-pop has become a global force. In 2020 the group **BTS** (방탄소년단) reached **number one on the US Billboard Hot 100** with their first all-English single, a first for a Korean act.
facts.korean_en: I like K-pop songs.
facts.note: **방탄소년단** means "Bulletproof Boy Scouts"; fans worldwide call the group by its English name, BTS.

facts.title: The idol trainee system
facts.fact: Most K-pop idols spend years as **trainees** at an entertainment company, studying singing and dancing before they debut. Groups like **BLACKPINK** trained for years before their first release.
facts.korean_en: That singer dances well.
facts.note: 춤을 추다 = "to dance" (literally "to dance a dance"). Many idols are valued as much for dancing as for singing.

facts.title: Trot, an old genre made new
facts.fact: **Trot** (트로트) is one of Korea's oldest popular music styles, with a sentimental sound. TV audition shows in recent years sparked a huge revival, making it popular again with both old and young fans.
facts.korean_en: Grandmother listens to trot often.
facts.note: **트로트** is loved across generations; TV singing contests gave it a big new audience.

facts.title: Pansori, a one-singer epic
facts.fact: **Pansori** (판소리) is a traditional form of musical storytelling in which a single singer performs a long tale, accompanied by one drummer. UNESCO recognized it as intangible cultural heritage in 2003.
facts.korean_en: Pansori is traditional Korean music.
facts.note: In **판소리**, the 소리 means "sound" or "voice"; the singer performs for hours with just a drum.

facts.title: Noraebang, the song room
facts.fact: A **noraebang** (노래방) is a private karaoke room rented by the hour. Singing together is a core social activity, often the last stop on a night out with friends or coworkers.
facts.korean_en: I go to a noraebang with friends.
facts.note: **노래방** literally means "song room" (노래 "song" + 방 "room").

facts.title: The gayageum, a string instrument
facts.fact: The **gayageum** (가야금) is a traditional Korean zither with twelve strings, plucked with the fingers. Its gentle sound has been played for well over a thousand years and still features in music today.
facts.korean_en: I am learning the gayageum.
facts.note: **가야금** is one of the best-known 국악기 (traditional Korean instruments).

facts.title: Korean hip-hop goes mainstream
facts.fact: Korean **hip-hop** grew from an underground scene into a mainstream genre, helped by long-running TV rap competitions. Many rappers now top the charts and shape Korean pop music.
facts.korean_en: I listen to Korean hip-hop.
facts.note: **힙합** is borrowed from the English "hip-hop"; rap is often called **랩**.

facts.title: The ballad, Korea's love song
facts.fact: The **ballad** (발라드) is one of Korea's most beloved pop genres, built on slow, emotional songs about love and heartbreak. Many of the country's biggest solo singers are known above all as ballad singers.
facts.korean_en: This ballad is really sad.
facts.note: **발라드** songs are famous for big, emotional choruses; 슬프다 ("to be sad") is a key word for describing them.

facts.title: K-indie and the Hongdae scene
facts.fact: Away from the big idol companies, Korea has a lively **indie** music scene. The **Hongdae** (홍대) area of Seoul, near a major art university, is famous for small live clubs and young indie bands.
facts.korean_en: I listened to indie music in Hongdae.
facts.note: **홍대** is short for 홍익대학교 (Hongik University); the neighborhood around it is a hub for clubs and street culture.

facts.title: Street busking on the weekend
facts.fact: Street performance, known by the borrowed word **busking** (버스킹), is common in lively areas of Seoul on weekend evenings. Singers and bands play for crowds on the sidewalk, and some later become well-known artists.
facts.korean_en: On the weekend I listened to singing on the street.
facts.note: **버스킹** comes from the English "busking"; you will often see it in 홍대 and other busy areas.

facts.title: Weekly TV music shows
facts.fact: Korean TV broadcasts several weekly **music shows** (음악방송) where idol groups perform their latest songs. Each show crowns a weekly winner, and winning is a proud moment for a group and its fans.
facts.korean_en: On Saturday I watch a music show.
facts.note: **음악방송** (음악 "music" + 방송 "broadcast") is where many groups promote a new release each week.

facts.title: Fan chants and lightsticks
facts.fact: K-pop fans show support with **fan chants**, shouting members' names at set points in a song, and with official **lightsticks** (응원봉). At a concert, thousands of lightsticks light up the dark arena together.
facts.korean_en: At the concert I wave a lightstick.
facts.note: **응원봉** = 응원 ("cheering") + 봉 ("stick"); each group often has its own uniquely shaped lightstick.

facts.title: OST, the drama soundtrack
facts.fact: Songs written for TV dramas, called **OST** (오에스티) from "original soundtrack," are hugely popular in Korea. A hit drama's love song can climb the charts just like a regular pop release.
facts.korean_en: I like this drama's soundtrack.
facts.note: Koreans usually say the English letters **OST** (오에스티); the songs are tied closely to popular dramas.

facts.title: Aegukga, the national anthem
facts.fact: **Aegukga** (애국가) is the national anthem of South Korea. Its title means "the song of love for one's country," and it is sung at school events and national ceremonies.
facts.korean_en: At the ceremony we sing the national anthem.
facts.note: **애국가** = 애국 ("patriotism, love of country") + 가 ("song"); 노래를 부르다 means "to sing a song."

facts.title: Arirang, a beloved folk song
facts.fact: **Arirang** (아리랑) is Korea's best-known folk song, with many regional versions sung for generations. UNESCO added it to its list of intangible cultural heritage in 2012.
facts.korean_en: Arirang is a famous folk song.
facts.note: **민요** means "folk song"; almost every Korean knows the melody of 아리랑.

facts.title: Samul nori, four percussion instruments
facts.fact: **Samul nori** (사물놀이) is an energetic percussion music played on **four instruments**: two drums and two gongs. It grew out of older farmers' band music and is now performed on stages worldwide.
facts.korean_en: The samul nori performance is very exciting.
facts.note: **사물놀이** means "playing of four things" — 사물 ("four objects") refers to the four instruments.

facts.title: The janggu, an hourglass drum
facts.fact: The **janggu** (장구) is a traditional drum with a narrow waist, shaped like an hourglass and played with a stick and the hand. It keeps the rhythm in many kinds of Korean folk and percussion music.
facts.korean_en: That person plays the janggu well.
facts.note: 북을 치다 / 장구를 치다 = "to play a drum"; 치다 is the verb used for striking instruments.

facts.title: The daegeum, a large bamboo flute
facts.fact: The **daegeum** (대금) is a large traditional flute made of bamboo, played sideways. A special membrane gives it a slightly buzzing tone, and it has long been important in Korean court and folk music.
facts.korean_en: The daegeum is made of bamboo.
facts.note: **대금** is a transverse (side-blown) flute; the 금 here means a wind or string instrument in many traditional names.

facts.title: The haegeum, a two-string fiddle
facts.fact: The **haegeum** (해금) is a traditional bowed string instrument with only **two strings**, held upright on the player's knee. Its expressive, voice-like sound appears in both traditional and modern Korean music.
facts.korean_en: The sound of the haegeum is really beautiful.
facts.note: The **해금** has just two strings, yet it can bend pitches smoothly like a human voice.

facts.title: Children's songs, dongyo
facts.fact: **Dongyo** (동요) are children's songs, taught at home and in school. Simple, cheerful songs about animals, seasons, and family help young children learn, and many adults still remember them fondly.
facts.korean_en: The children sing children's songs.
facts.note: **동요** = 동 ("child") + 요 ("song"); these are often the first Korean songs learners pick up.

facts.title: Summer music festivals
facts.fact: Korea hosts many outdoor **music festivals**, especially in summer. Rock and pop festivals draw big crowds, and some take place at the beach or in the mountains over a long weekend.
facts.korean_en: In summer I went to a music festival.
facts.note: **축제** means "festival"; 음악 축제 is a music festival, a popular summer outing.

facts.title: The digital music chart
facts.fact: In Korea, song popularity is tracked mostly through **streaming and downloads** on music apps. Hitting number one on a major real-time digital chart is a key goal for artists and their fans.
facts.korean_en: That song reached number one.
facts.note: **1위** is read "일 위" and means "first place"; 위 marks rank. Fans work hard to push a song to 1위.
